Given some categorized constants in the:
```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
clients("Aimee"; "Ginger"; "Freda"; "Hannah").
price(150; 160; 170; 180).
masseuse("Lynda"; "Nancy"; "Teri"; "Whitney").

% Define the match relation: client, price, masseuse.
match(C, P, M) :- clients(C), price(P), masseuse(M).

% Constraints based on given predicates.
% Hannah must be matched with some price PH.
match("Hannah", PH, _) :- price(PH).

% Teri must be matched with some price PT.
match(_, PT, "Teri") :- price(PT).

% The price for Hannah must be greater than the price for Teri.
:- match("Hannah", PH, _), match(_, PT, "Teri"), PH <= PT.

% Each client is matched with exactly one price and one masseuse.
{match(C, P, M): price(P), masseuse(M)}=1 :- clients(C).
```

% Define the constants in each category.
companies("Permias"; "Rubicorp"; "Techtrin"; "Ubersplore").
names("Cornick"; "Dreadco"; "Foltron"; "Worul").
months(1; 2; 3; 4).

% Define the single predicate to model the full assignment: company launches rocket in a given month.
launch(C, R, M) :- companies(C), names(R), months(M).

% Ensure each company is assigned exactly one rocket and one month.
{launch(C, R, M) : names(R), months(M)} = 1 :- companies(C).

% Ensure each rocket is launched by exactly one company in exactly one month.
{launch(C, R, M) : companies(C), months(M)} = 1 :- names(R).

% Ensure each month has exactly one launch (one company, one rocket).
{launch(C, R, M) : companies(C), names(R)} = 1 :- months(M).
```

% Define the constants in each category.
miners("Fred Fletcher"; "Gil Gonzalez"; "Ivan Ingram"; "Jack Jacobs").
ounces(11; 14; 17; 20).
claims("Belle Hart"; "Culver Gorge"; "Fuller Rise"; "York River").

% Define the minimal predicate that relates all three categories.
% This single predicate captures the full assignment: miner M at claim C found O ounces.
mine(M, O, C) :- miners(M), ounces(O), claims(C).

% Ensure each miner is assigned exactly one claim and one amount.
{mine(M, O, C) : ounces(O), claims(C)} = 1 :- miners(M).

% Ensure each amount is used exactly once (if uniqueness is implied by context).
{mine(M, O, C) : miners(M), claims(C)} = 1 :- ounces(O).

% Ensure each claim is assigned exactly one miner and one amount.
{mine(M, O, C) : miners(M), ounces(O)} = 1 :- claims(C).
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
players("Awad"; "Daregh"; "Gardelli"; "Rothvum").
years(1976; 1979; 1982; 1985).
positions("center back"; "center forward"; "goalie"; "sweeper").

% Define the main predicate: player(P, Y, Pos) meaning player P started in year Y and plays position Pos.
player(P, Y, Pos) :- players(P), years(Y), positions(Pos).

% Each player has exactly one year and one position.
{player(P, Y, Pos) : years(Y), positions(Pos)} = 1 :- players(P).

% Each year has exactly one player and one position.
{player(P, Y, Pos) : players(P), positions(Pos)} = 1 :- years(Y).

% Each position has exactly one player and one year.
{player(P, Y, Pos) : players(P), years(Y)} = 1 :- positions(Pos).

% Clue 1: Daregh started 6 years before the goalie.
:- player(Daregh, Y1, _), player(P, Y2, "goalie"), Y2 = Y1 + 6.

% Clue 2: The player who started in 1982 was either the goalie or Daregh.
:- player(P, 1982, _), P != "Daregh", not player(P, 1982, "goalie").

% Clue 3: Awad began playing in 1985.
:- not player("Awad", 1985, _).

% Clue 4: The player who started in 1976 was the sweeper.
:- player(P, 1976, Pos), Pos != "sweeper".

% Clue 5: Gardelli was either the center back or the player who started in 1979.
:- player("Gardelli", Y, Pos), Y != 1979, Pos != "center back".
```

% Clue 1: Zachary wore the aquamarine shirt.
assign("Zachary", "aquamarine", T) :- time(T).

% Clue 2: The contestant in the cyan shirt finished 1 minute after Franklin.
:- assign("Franklin", _, T1), assign(R, "cyan", T2), T2 = T1 + 1.

% Clue 3: Zachary finished 2 minutes after Salvador.
:- assign("Salvador", _, T1), assign("Zachary", _, T2), T2 = T1 + 2.

% Clue 4: The contestant in the maroon shirt finished 1 minute before Salvador.
:- assign(R, "maroon", T1), assign("Salvador", _, T2), T1 = T2 - 1.

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
rankings(1; 2; 3; 4).
teams("Color Blinds"; "Night Ninjas"; "Oil Crew"; "Target Bombs").
colors("green"; "orange"; "purple"; "white").

% Define the assignment predicate: each team has one color and one ranking.
assign(T, C, R) :- teams(T), colors(C), rankings(R).

% Each team has exactly one color and one ranking.
{assign(T, C, R) : colors(C), rankings(R)} = 1 :- teams(T).

% Each color is used exactly once.
{assign(T, C, R) : teams(T), rankings(R)} = 1 :- colors(C).

% Each ranking is used exactly once.
{assign(T, C, R) : teams(T), colors(C)} = 1 :- rankings(R).

% Clue 1: The Color Blinds finished first.
assign("Color Blinds", _, 1).

% Clue 2: The Color Blinds was ranked 1 place ahead of the green team.
% So if Color Blinds is rank R, green team is rank R+1.
% Thus, Color Blinds cannot be rank 4, and green team cannot be rank 1.
% From clue 1, Color Blinds is rank 1 → green team is rank 2.
assign(T, "green", 2) :- assign("Color Blinds", _, 1), teams(T).

% Clue 3: The Target Bombs uses white paintballs.
assign("Target Bombs", "white", _).

% Clue 4: Of the Oil Crew and the orange team, one finished third and the other finished second.
% So either:
%   (Oil Crew → 3, orange team → 2) OR (Oil Crew → 2, orange team → 3)
% We express this as:
%   assign("Oil Crew", _, 2) ↔ assign(T, "orange", 3)   [but not directly]
% Instead, use disjunction:
%   (assign("Oil Crew", _, 2) ∧ assign(T, "orange", 3)) ∨ (assign("Oil Crew", _, 3) ∧ assign(T, "orange", 2))
% But we need to express this without assuming which team has which color.

% We can write:
%   If Oil Crew is 2, then orange team is 3; if Oil Crew is 3, then orange team is 2.
% But we don't know which team has orange color.

% So we use: for the team with color "orange", it must be either 2 or 3, and Oil Crew must be the other.

% Let’s define: the team with color "orange" is not Oil Crew, or they are, but their rankings are different.

% Better: we use a disjunction over the two possibilities.

% We introduce: the orange team is not Oil Crew → then they are different teams → so we can write:
%   (assign("Oil Crew", _, 2) ∧ assign(T, "orange", 3)) ∨ (assign("Oil Crew", _, 3) ∧ assign(T, "orange", 2))

% But we can’t use T in a disjunction like that.

% Instead, we use:
%   There exists a team T such that T ≠ "Oil Crew" and assign(T, "orange", R), and the ranking of Oil Crew is the other one.

% But we can do:

%   assign("Oil Crew", _, 2) :- assign(T, "orange", 3), teams(T), T != "Oil Crew".
%   assign("Oil Crew", _, 3) :- assign(T, "orange", 2), teams(T), T != "Oil Crew".

% But that’s not correct — it’s not bidirectional.

% Correct way: the two teams (Oil Crew and the orange team) must have rankings 2 and 3, in some order.

% So:
%   (assign("Oil Crew", _, 2) ∧ assign(T, "orange", 3)) ∨ (assign("Oil Crew", _, 3) ∧ assign(T, "orange", 2))

% We can express this as:
%   assign("Oil Crew", _, 2) ∨ assign("Oil Crew", _, 3)   % already enforced by cardinality
%   But we need to enforce that one is 2 and the other is 3.

% So: the ranking of Oil Crew and the ranking of the orange team must be {2,3}, and they are not the same team.

% We can write:
%   Let T be the team with color "orange", T ≠ "Oil Crew", and {rank(T), rank(Oil Crew)} = {2,3}

% But we can’t directly compare ranks.

% Instead, we use:
%   (assign("Oil Crew", _, 2) ∧ assign(T, "orange", 3)) ∨ (assign("Oil Crew", _, 3) ∧ assign(T, "orange", 2))

% But we can’t use T in a disjunction unless we define it.

% So we do:
%   For all T, if T is the orange team and T ≠ "Oil Crew", then the rankings must be swapped.

% But better: use a choice rule to enforce the disjunction.

% We can write:
%   assign("Oil Crew", _, 2) :- assign(T, "orange", 3), teams(T), T != "Oil Crew".
%   assign("Oil Crew", _, 3) :- assign(T, "orange", 2), teams(T), T != "Oil Crew".

% But that’s not sufficient — it only forces Oil Crew to be 2 or 3 if the orange team is 3 or 2, but not the reverse.

% Instead, we use:
%   The orange team is not Oil Crew → then their rankings must be 2 and 3.

% But we don’t know which team has the orange color.

% So we define:
%   Let O be the team with color "orange"
%   Then O ≠ "Oil Crew" → and {rank(O), rank("Oil Crew")} = {2,3}

% We can express this with:
%   assign("Oil Crew", _, 2) :- assign(T, "orange", 3), teams(T), T != "Oil Crew".
%   assign("Oil Crew", _, 3) :- assign(T, "orange", 2), teams(T), T != "Oil Crew".

% But this is not symmetric.

% Better: we use a disjunction over the two cases.

% We can use:
%   (assign("Oil Crew", _, 2) ∧ assign(T, "orange", 3)) ∨ (assign("Oil Crew", _, 3) ∧ assign(T, "orange", 2))

% But we can’t write that directly.

% Instead, we use:
%   For each team T, if T is not "Oil Crew", then:
%       if assign(T, "orange", 3), then assign("Oil Crew",

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
fliers("Katie"; "Neal"; "Troy"; "Yolanda").
months(1; 2; 3; 4).
lucky_charms("coin"; "rabbit's foot"; "talisman"; "wishbone").

% Define the assignment predicate: assign(F, M, C) means flier F leaves in month M and carries lucky charm C.
assign(F, M, C) :- fliers(F), months(M), lucky_charms(C).

% Each flier is assigned exactly one month and one lucky charm.
{assign(F, M, C) : months(M), lucky_charms(C)} = 1 :- fliers(F).

% Each month is assigned to exactly one flier.
{assign(F, M, C) : fliers(F), lucky_charms(C)} = 1 :- months(M).

% Each lucky charm is assigned to exactly one flier.
{assign(F, M, C) : fliers(F), months(M)} = 1 :- lucky_charms(C).

% Clue 1: The passenger with the wishbone is either Troy or Neal.
:- not (assign("Troy", _, "wishbone") ; assign("Neal", _, "wishbone")).

% Clue 2: The four fliers are the passenger with the rabbit's foot, the passenger leaving in April, Yolanda, and the passenger leaving in February.
% This means that these four roles are all distinct and cover all fliers.
% So: the rabbit's foot holder ≠ April flier, ≠ Yolanda, ≠ February flier
% and April flier ≠ Yolanda, ≠ February flier, and Yolanda ≠ February flier.
% But since each role is unique and there are four fliers, we enforce that the four roles are distinct.

% Let's define the four roles:
% - rabbit's foot holder
% - April flier
% - Yolanda
% - February flier

% We must have that:
% - The rabbit's foot holder is not Yolanda
% - The rabbit's foot holder is not the April flier
% - The rabbit's foot holder is not the February flier
% - The April flier is not Yolanda
% - The April flier is not the February flier
% - Yolanda is not the February flier

% So we enforce:
:- assign(F, _, "rabbit's foot"), F = "Yolanda".
:- assign(F, 4, _), F = "Yolanda".
:- assign(F, 4, _), F = "Neal", assign("Neal", _, "rabbit's foot").
:- assign(F, 4, _), F = "Troy", assign("Troy", _, "rabbit's foot").
:- assign(F, 4, _), F = "Katie", assign("Katie", _, "rabbit's foot").
:- assign(F, 2, _), F = "Yolanda".
:- assign(F, 4, _), F = "February", assign("February", _, _). % This is not needed, just use direct values.

% Better: enforce that the rabbit's foot holder is not Yolanda, not the April flier, not the February flier.
:- assign(F, _, "rabbit's foot"), F = "Yolanda".
:- assign(F, 4, _), assign(F, _, "rabbit's foot").
:- assign(F, 2, _), assign(F, _, "rabbit's foot").

% Similarly, April flier ≠ Yolanda, April flier ≠ February flier
:- assign("Yolanda", 4, _).
:- assign(F, 4, _), assign(F, 2, _).

% Yolanda ≠ February flier
:- assign("Yolanda", 2, _).

% But wait — the clue says the four fliers are: rabbit's foot, April, Yolanda, February — meaning these are the four distinct identities.
% So we can also interpret it as: the set of fliers is exactly {rabbit's foot holder, April flier, Yolanda, February flier}
% Since there are four fliers, and four roles, and all are distinct, we must have:
% - rabbit's foot holder ≠ April flier
% - rabbit's foot holder ≠ Yolanda
% - rabbit's foot holder ≠ February flier
% - April flier ≠ Yolanda
% - April flier ≠ February flier
% - Yolanda ≠ February flier

% So we enforce all pairwise inequalities:

% Rabbit's foot holder ≠ Yolanda
:- assign("Yolanda", _, "rabbit's foot").

% Rabbit's foot holder ≠ April flier
:- assign(F, 4, _), assign(F, _, "rabbit's foot").

% Rabbit's foot holder ≠ February flier
:- assign(F, 2, _), assign(F, _, "rabbit's foot").

% April flier ≠ Yolanda
:- assign("Yolanda", 4, _).

% April flier ≠ February flier
:- assign(F, 4, _), assign(F, 2, _).

% Yolanda ≠ February flier
:- assign("Yolanda", 2, _).
```

% Define the constants in each category.
countries("Dominica"; "Jordan"; "Venezuela"; "Zimbabwe").
gold_medals(1; 2; 3; 4).
silver_medals(2; 5; 6; 8).

% For each country C, it is matched with exactly 1 gold medal G and 1 silver medal S.
{medals(C, G, S): gold_medals(G), silver_medals(S)}=1 :- countries(C).
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
employee(1; 2; 3; 4).
riders(50; 75; 100; 125).
section("blue"; "green"; "red"; "yellow").

% Define the minimal set of predicates to capture all relations.
% We use a single ternary predicate to represent the assignment of employee, section, and riders.
assign(E, S, R) :- employee(E), section(S), riders(R).

% Ensure each employee is assigned to exactly one section and one riders count.
{assign(E, S, R) : section(S), riders(R)} = 1 :- employee(E).

% Ensure each section is assigned to exactly one employee and one riders count.
{assign(E, S, R) : employee(E), riders(R)} = 1 :- section(S).

% Ensure each riders count is assigned to exactly one employee and one section.
{assign(E, S, R) : employee(E), section(S)} = 1 :- riders(R).
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
players("Charles"; "Evan"; "Karl"; "Vincent").
games(8; 9; 10; 11).
positions("center field"; "first base"; "shortstop"; "third base").

% For each player, assign exactly one position and one number of games.
{assign(Player, Position, Games): positions(Position), games(Games)}=1 :- players(Player).
```
